html:
Karl Fredrik, born April 19th (Old Style)/April 29th (New Style) 1700 in Stockholm, and died June 18, 1739 in Rohlfshagen, was the Duke of Holstein-Gottorp from 1702 and a Swedish prince. He was the son of Frederick IV of Holstein-Gottorp and Hedvig Sofia, daughter of King Charles XI of Sweden. According to the Act of Union of 1650, which was superseded by the Act of Succession of Norrköping, he was a Swedish prince and, after the death of Charles XII, a claimant to the Swedish throne. He married Anna Petrovna, the daughter of Tsar Peter the Great of Russia, in 1725.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life === Karl Fredrik was born in Stockholm, where his parents had moved the year before his birth at the invitation of his uncle, Charles XII of Sweden. He subsequently grew up at the Swedish court. Upon his father's death during the Battle of Kliszów on July 19, 1702, he became Duke of Holstein-Gottorp under the guardianship of his mother, but remained in Sweden. After his mother's death in 1708, he was raised by his maternal grandmother, Queen Dowager Hedvig Eleonora of Sweden. She was his favorite and he was described as spoiled, indulged, passive, and indolent. His mother reportedly combined gentleness with severity and raised him strictly, while Hedvig Eleonora allowed him anything he wanted.

=== Career in Sweden ===

During Charles XII's absence, two factions emerged: one supporting Charles Frederick as the heir to the throne, and another advocating for his aunt, Ulrika Eleonora. Charles Frederick was supported by the Queen Dowager. He met his uncle, Charles XII, for the first time, as did his aunt's husband, Frederick of Hesse, upon the latter's return to Sweden in 1716. Charles Frederick was declared an adult ("declared major") in 1717 and subsequently received command over certain units of the Swedish army.

Charles Frederick had a strained relationship with Ulrika Eleonora, whose supporters highlighted his "disrespect" and arrogance as irresponsible traits that made him unsuitable to inherit the crown before her. However, he did reconcile with her husband, Frederick.

At the time of Charles XII's death, both Charles Frederick and Frederick were in Tistedalen. While Frederick immediately helped his wife seize the throne, Charles Frederick isolated himself in his tent to mourn, thus missing the opportunity to take the initiative in the power struggle.

In 1718, Charles Frederick was bypassed in the Swedish line of succession by his aunt, Ulrika Eleonora. She argued that his mother, Charles Frederick's elder sister, had not "obtained the consent of the Estates" for her marriage. However, Charles Frederick's faction maintained that this had not been necessary due to the introduction of absolutism in Sweden under his grandfather. When Charles Frederick met Ulrika Eleonora for the first time since she had gained power, the Chancellor Arvid Horn was forced to persuade him to address her as Queen. He requested the title of Royal Highness and thereby be recognized as the heir to the throne. When this was denied, and when Frederick of Hesse instead received this title, he chose to leave the country.

In 1723, the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) granted him the Swedish title of Royal Highness, but the pro-Russian policies subsequently pursued in his name by the Holstein faction blocked both his path and that of his son to the throne. After the deaths of both Charles Frederick and Ulrika Eleonora, his cousin, Adolf Frederick, from the same Oldenburg dynasty, was elected as the Swedish Crown Prince.

=== Later Life ===

In May 1719, Karl Fredrik left Sweden and traveled to his uncle in Hamburg, as Holstein-Gottorp had been occupied by Denmark since 1713. That same year, he successfully ended the occupation after appealing to Emperor Charles VI of the Holy Roman Empire.

He then traveled to Russia at the invitation of Empress Catherine. For many years, negotiations had been underway regarding a marriage between him and the Tsar's daughter, Anna. Initially, Tsar Peter had demanded that Karl Fredrik be assured of the Swedish succession, but this requirement was abandoned. It is said that Anna was not enthusiastic, as Karl Fredrik was at that time notorious for his relationships with prostitutes. Karl Fredrik was officially betrothed to Anna and treated as the Tsar's future son-in-law.

Around 1721-1722, Karl Fredrik helped several hundred Swedish prisoners of war (now released following the Treaty of Nystad) return home to Sweden from Siberia and other parts of Russia. He gave them money from his own pocket to supplement their travel funds in rubles, which they sometimes received upon their release.

Upon Tsar Peter's death in 1725, he was given a court, palace, allowance, and a seat on the Russian council by his future mother-in-law, Catherine I. The wedding took place that same year. In 1727, the couple traveled to Holstein-Gottorp, where Anna died in 1728 during childbirth with the couple's son, Peter. After his son's birth, Karl Fredrik remained in Holstein-Gottorp and spent the rest of his life planning for his son's future as a Russian monarch. He supported his followers in Sweden but without enthusiasm, and he did not actively participate in governing Holstein-Gottorp.

Karl Fredrik's and Anna's marriage can be seen as an expression of reconciliation between the Palatinate House in Sweden and the Romanov dynasty in Russia, after many years of war. Their son, Peter, became Karl Fredrik's successor as Duke of Holstein in 1739.

How certain are we about this?

Information about figures from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary publications, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political sympathies.
tldr: Karl Fredrik was a Swedish prince who, for a time, was one of the individuals who could have potentially become King of Sweden. He left the country and married a Russian princess after losing his chance to ascend to the Swedish throne.
lattlast:
Karl Fredrik was born in Stockholm and grew up near the Swedish royal court. Because he was the son of a Swedish princess and a German duke, he was considered one of the potential heirs to the throne after King Karl XII. However, there was a major conflict about who would become the next king, and Karl Fredrik lost this power struggle to his aunt.

After losing his chance to become the Swedish king, he left Sweden in 1719. He traveled to Germany and later to Russia. There, he married the daughter of the Russian Tsar, which was a way for the two countries to demonstrate their desire for friendship after many years of war.

During his final years, he lived in the region known as Holstein-Gottorp. He spent his time planning for his son's future and helping his supporters in Sweden, even though he no longer held any power within the Swedish kingdom.
